content:
# Titus 3 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

**Verse 1** -- PNEUMA adds "the believers" as the object of "Remind" and restructures the infinitive chain: "to submit to rulers and authorities, to be obedient, and to be ready to do whatever is good." EMET and LOGOS preserve the Greek infinitive sequence literally. PNEUMA also renders "every good work" as "whatever is good," losing the noun ΕΡΓΟΝ. **Resolution:** Followed EMET/LOGOS.

**Verse 2** -- Three-way divergence on the key terms:
- ΒΛΑΣΦΗΜΕΙΝ: EMET "to blaspheme no one" | LOGOS "to speak evil of no one" | PNEUMA "not slander anyone"
- ΑΜΑΧΟΥΣ: EMET "non-contentious" | LOGOS "peaceable" | PNEUMA "avoid quarreling"
- ΕΠΙΕΙΚΕΙΣ: EMET "gentle" | LOGOS "gentle" | PNEUMA "considerate of others"
- ΠΡΑΥΤΗΤΑ: EMET "meekness" | LOGOS "meekness" | PNEUMA "genuine humility"
- PNEUMA restructures the verse into separate sentences.
**Resolution:** "To speak evil of no one" (LOGOS) chosen over the overly literal "blaspheme" (EMET) since the Greek ΒΛΑΣΦΗΜΕΙΝ has a broader range than English "blaspheme." "Peaceable" (LOGOS) preferred over "non-contentious" (EMET). "Gentle" and "meekness" retained per EMET/LOGOS agreement. PNEUMA's restructuring rejected.

**Verse 3** -- Divergences:
- EMET: "we also were once" | LOGOS: "we ourselves also were once" | PNEUMA: "there was a time when we ourselves were"
- ΠΛΑΝΩΜΕΝΟΙ: EMET/LOGOS "being led astray" | PNEUMA "we were misled and enslaved"
- ΔΟΥΛΕΥΟΝΤΕΣ: EMET/LOGOS "serving various desires and pleasures" | PNEUMA "enslaved by all kinds of passions and pleasures"
- ΕΠΙΘΥΜΙΑΙΣ: EMET/LOGOS "desires" | PNEUMA "passions"
- ΣΤΥΓΗΤΟΙ: EMET/LOGOS "hateful" | PNEUMA "people hated us as much as we hated them"
- PNEUMA converts participial clauses into independent sentences.
**Resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "We ourselves also" is more emphatic and faithful to the Greek's inclusive emphasis. "Being led astray" and "serving" preserved as participles. PNEUMA's expansion of ΣΤΥΓΗΤΟΙ into a reciprocal clause is interpretive overreach.

**Verse 4** -- Divergences:
- ΦΙΛΑΝΘΡΩΠΙΑ: EMET/LOGOS "love-for-mankind" | PNEUMA "love"
- Word order: EMET "the Savior of us, God" | LOGOS "God our Savior" | PNEUMA "God our Savior"
- PNEUMA merges verses 4-5 into a single unit.
**Resolution:** "Love-for-mankind" retained per EMET/LOGOS -- this preserves the distinctive Greek compound ΦΙΛΑΝΘΡΩΠΙΑ, which is not mere "love" (ΑΓΑΠΗ). "God our Savior" adopted over EMET's hyper-literal "the Savior of us, God" for natural English word order. Verses kept separate (rejecting PNEUMA's merger).

**Verse 5** -- Significant divergences:
- Structure: EMET "not from works which are in righteousness which we did" | LOGOS "not by works which we did in righteousness" | PNEUMA "not because of any righteous things we had done"
- ΛΟΥΤΡΟΥ ΠΑΛΙΓΓΕΝΕΣΙΑΣ: EMET "washing of regeneration" | LOGOS "washing of regeneration" | PNEUMA "the washing that gave us new birth"
- ΑΝΑΚΑΙΝΩΣΕΩΣ: EMET "renewal of the Holy Spirit" | LOGOS "renewal of the Holy Spirit" | PNEUMA "renewal by the Holy Spirit"
- Pronouns: EMET "the mercy of him, he saved us" | LOGOS "his mercy he saved us" | PNEUMA "his own mercy"
**Resolution:** "Not by works of righteousness which we did" synthesizes EMET's fidelity to the Greek prepositional phrase with LOGOS's smoother English. "Washing of regeneration" retained per EMET/LOGOS agreement -- PNEUMA's paraphrase dissolves the theologically loaded term ΠΑΛΙΓΓΕΝΕΣΙΑ. "Renewal of the Holy Spirit" kept, preserving the genitive ambiguity of the Greek.

**Verse 6** -- Divergences:
- Relative pronoun: EMET "which" | LOGOS "whom" | PNEUMA "whom"
- ΠΛΟΥΣΙΩΣ: EMET "richly" | LOGOS "richly" | PNEUMA "generously"
- Name form: EMET "Yeshua Christos" | LOGOS "Jesus Christ" | PNEUMA "Jesus Christ"
- Possessive: EMET "the Savior of us" | LOGOS "our Savior" | PNEUMA "our Savior"
**Resolution:** "Whom" adopted (LOGOS/PNEUMA) -- the antecedent is the Holy Spirit (personal pronoun appropriate). "Richly" retained per EMET/LOGOS. "Jesus Christ" adopted as standard English form over EMET's transliteration. "Our Savior" preferred over "the Savior of us."

**Verse 8** -- Multiple divergences:
- ΠΙΣΤΟΣ Ο ΛΟΓΟΣ: EMET "Faithful is the word" | LOGOS "Faithful is the word" | PNEUMA "This is a trustworthy saying"
- ΔΙΑΒΕΒΑΙΟΥΣΘΑΙ: EMET "affirm strongly" | LOGOS "affirm strongly" | PNEUMA "insist on these things firmly"
- ΠΕΠΙΣΤΕΥΚΟΤΕΣ: EMET "the ones having believed" | LOGOS "those who have believed" | PNEUMA "those who have put their trust in"
- ΠΡΟΙΣΤΑΣΘΑΙ ΚΑΛΩΝ ΕΡΓΩΝ: EMET "to stand before in good works" | LOGOS "to devote themselves to good works" | PNEUMA "devote themselves to doing what is good"
- Final clause: EMET "profitable to men" | LOGOS "profitable for men" | PNEUMA "beneficial for everyone"
**Resolution:** "Faithful is the word" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Greek predicate-first emphasis, preferred over PNEUMA's paraphrase. "Devote themselves to good works" (LOGOS) chosen for ΠΡΟΙΣΤΗΜΙ -- EMET's "stand before" is too cryptic for English readers, while PNEUMA's "doing what is good" loses the noun. "Profitable for men" adopted.

**Verse 9** -- Divergences:
- Structure: EMET places "avoid" at the end | LOGOS/PNEUMA front "avoid"/"stay away from"
- ΖΗΤΗΣΕΙΣ: EMET "questionings" | LOGOS "controversies" | PNEUMA "controversies"
- ΜΑΧΑΣ: EMET "contentions" | LOGOS "quarrels" | PNEUMA "fights"
- ΜΑΤΑΙΟΙ: EMET "vain" | LOGOS "worthless" | PNEUMA "get you nowhere"
- PNEUMA adds "genealogical debates" and "because they are pointless."
**Resolution:** Fronted "avoid" per LOGOS for clarity. "Controversies" adopted per LOGOS/PNEUMA over EMET's archaic "questionings." "Quarrels" preferred. "Worthless" (LOGOS) chosen over "vain" (EMET, archaic) and PNEUMA's paraphrase.

**Verse 10** -- Key divergence on ΑΙΡΕΤΙΚΟΝ:
- EMET: "A heretical man" | LOGOS: "A factious man" | PNEUMA: "If someone is causing divisions among you, warn them once, and then warn them a second time. After that, have nothing more to do with them."
- PNEUMA completely restructures the verse into conditional sentences, dissolving the compact Greek imperative.
**Resolution:** "Factious" (LOGOS) adopted -- all three translators' notes agree that ΑΙΡΕΤΙΚΟΝ in its first-century context denotes faction-making, not later doctrinal heresy. EMET's "heretical" is anachronistic. PNEUMA's paraphrase, while clear, loses the verse's compact force.

**Verse 12** -- Divergences:
- Name forms: EMET "Tychikos" / "Nikopolis" | LOGOS/PNEUMA "Tychicus" / "Nicopolis"
- ΣΠΟΥΔΑΣΟΝ: EMET "be diligent" | LOGOS "make haste" | PNEUMA "do your best"
- ΠΑΡΑΧΕΙΜΑΣΑΙ: EMET "to winter there" | LOGOS "to spend the winter there" | PNEUMA "to spend the winter there"
**Resolution:** Standard English name forms "Tychicus" and "Nicopolis" adopted per LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Make haste" (LOGOS) preferred -- captures the urgency of ΣΠΟΥΔΑΖΩ better than "be diligent." "Spend the winter" preferred for clarity.

**Verse 13** -- Divergences:
- ΠΡΟΠΕΜΨΟΝ: EMET "diligently send forward" | LOGOS "send on their way diligently" | PNEUMA "Do everything you can to help ... on their journey"
- Final clause: EMET "that nothing may be lacking to them" | LOGOS "so that nothing may be lacking for them" | PNEUMA "see that they have everything they need"
**Resolution:** "Send on their way diligently" (LOGOS) adopted as the clearest formal rendering of ΠΡΟΠΕΜΠΩ. PNEUMA's expansion is too free. "So that nothing may be lacking for them" per LOGOS.

**Verse 14** -- Divergences:
- Subject: EMET "ours" | LOGOS "our people" | PNEUMA "Our people"
- ΠΡΟΙΣΤΑΣΘΑΙ ΚΑΛΩΝ ΕΡΓΩΝ: EMET "to stand before in good works" | LOGOS "to devote themselves to good works" | PNEUMA "to devote themselves to doing what is good"
- ΑΝΑΓΚΑΙΑΣ ΧΡΕΙΑΣ: EMET "necessary needs" | LOGOS "pressing needs" | PNEUMA "urgent needs"
- Final clause: EMET "that they may not be unfruitful" | LOGOS "so that they may not be unfruitful" | PNEUMA "so that their lives will not be unfruitful"
**Resolution:** "Our people" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is clearer than EMET's bare "ours." "Devote themselves to good works" again chosen for ΠΡΟΙΣΤΗΜΙ. "Pressing needs" (LOGOS) is a strong rendering of ΑΝΑΓΚΑΙΑΣ ΧΡΕΙΑΣ -- more vivid than "necessary needs" without PNEUMA's interpretive shift to "urgent." PNEUMA's addition of "their lives" is unwarranted by the Greek.

**Verse 15** -- Minor divergences:
- EMET: "All the ones with me greet you. Greet the ones loving us in faith. The grace with all of you." | LOGOS: "All those with me greet you. Greet those who love us in the faith. Grace be with you all." | PNEUMA: "Everyone here with me sends you greetings. Give our greetings to those who love us in the faith. Grace be with all of you."
- Article: EMET "in faith" | LOGOS "in the faith" | PNEUMA "in the faith"
- Benediction form: EMET "The grace with all of you" | LOGOS "Grace be with you all" | PNEUMA "Grace be with all of you"
**Resolution:** "In the faith" adopted (LOGOS/PNEUMA) -- the article ΤΗ is present in Sinaiticus, confirmed by manuscript inspection. "Grace be with you all" (LOGOS) is the smoothest rendering. PNEUMA's "Everyone here" and "sends you greetings" / "Give our greetings" is too free.

-- SOPHIA
